title: ‘The Discovery’ Director Charlie McDowell | The Big Picture (Ep. 7)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to writer-director Charlie McDowell on the day The Discovery lands on Netflix: the film's high-concept afterlife premise, casting Robert Redford, why the ending came before the middle, and what a straight-to-Netflix release does to a movie.

films.title: The Discovery
films.note: McDowell's Netflix sci-fi drama: a scientist (Robert Redford) proves the afterlife exists and millions start killing themselves to reach it. He calls it a character study with a high concept as backdrop, a love story and a father-son story, written end-first, with Redford cast off an agent's availability list after he turned out to be a fan of The One I Love. Shot by European DP Sturla (his first American film) for a dark look. Cast: Jason Segel as the audience's eyes, Rooney Mara, Jesse Plemons, Riley Keough.

films.title: The One I Love
films.note: McDowell's first feature, shot in 15 days at a house in Ojai with Mark Duplass and Elisabeth Moss: a couple on a marital retreat who discover something strange about themselves. The twist was so protected by critics that reviewers refused to spoil it, which McDowell counts as the ultimate compliment.

films.title: Arrival
films.note: His favorite film of the prior year, cited as the model for a movie that plays completely differently on a second viewing.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: Brought up via a recent Jordan Peele appearance on the show. Fennessey asks whether McDowell plants the same Easter-egg-for-a-second-watch details on purpose; he says it isn't conscious, but the actors are directed knowing where it ends up.

In development (not films): Zero K (Don DeLillo limited series for FX)
